meta.title: E93 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 3 Series E93 engine — N47, M57, N57, N52, N54, N55. Real UK costs, timing‑chain risks, convertible‑specific labour access, and honest reliability data from 20+ vetted BMW specialists.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series E93 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E93 engine typically costs £2,500–£7,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume N47D20C diesel sits at £3,200–£5,500, while the N54/N55 sixes are £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500, and the E93's additional structural bracing may add labour time. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 3 Series E93 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M57D30 (pre-LCI 325d/330d, 2007–2010) — a cast-iron-block six with a genuine 300,000-mile reputation and none of the rear-chain risk that defines the later N47 and N57. On the petrol side, the N52B30 is the best naturally aspirated option — no turbos, no HPFP, just smooth six-cylinder power. The N55 in the LCI 335i is also a solid choice.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 3 Series E93?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear on the N47D20C diesel, HPFP and injector failure on the N54 335i, thermostat and swirl flap wear on the M57D30, and — unrelated to the engine — folding hardtop mechanism wear on all E93s. The convertible's roof is the most common non-engine repair.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 3 Series E93 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and which side of the March 2010 facelift your car sits on. Pre-LCI 330d buyers get the bulletproof M57; post-LCI buyers get the improved but chain-affected N57. The N47 diesel four carries this generation's biggest risk regardless of facelift. The N52 petrol six is the most dependable petrol choice.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 3 Series E93 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre-2013 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected N54 335i without HPFP history is the second. On the E93, also ensure the folding roof mechanism has been serviced — it's expensive to repair.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E93 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N47/N57), long cranking or limp mode (N54 HPFP), or blue smoke and rough idle (any high-mileage engine) are the key warning signs. On the E93 specifically, also watch for roof operation faults — they're not engine-related but can be expensive. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 3 Series E93 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £12,000 E93, a £4,000 N47 recon is 33% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon is 80% — that crosses our 70% BMW threshold, so we'd advise walking away. E93 convertibles command a premium over saloons, so value the car first.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 3 Series E93 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M57 diesel six can exceed 300,000 miles with proper servicing. The N52 petrol six is similarly durable. The N47 and N57's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, either can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles. The N55 is also durable with proper maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E90, E91, E92 and E93?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the fifth-generation 3 Series platform and most engines. The E90 is the 4-door saloon (2005–2011), the E91 is the Touring estate (2005–2012), the E92 is the 2-door coupé (2006–2013), and the E93 is the 2-door convertible (2007–2013). The E93 has a folding metal hardtop and is approximately 200 kg heavier than the E92. Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 3 Series E93 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on N47/M57/N57 units. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date (pre- or post-March 2010) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 3 Series E93 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E93 convertible — from the naturally aspirated N52 sixes to the twin-turbo N54 and the post-2010 N55 switch, alongside the full range of diesel fours and sixes that define UK ownership. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the convertible-specific labour access that sets this body apart from the E90 saloon. Backed by 256+ real UK E9X enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 3 Series E93 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E93 convertible specifically — including the mid-cycle N54-to-N55 switch and the M57-to-N57 diesel transition that most guides miss.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N47D20A/C: 95% confidence — 1,450 JLR-wide [EM-VERIFIED] figures; reliability rating inherited from the Model Page. M57D30: 90% confidence — 830 JLR-wide [EM-VERIFIED] figures; reliability rating inherited from the Model Page. N52, N54 and N55: Expert-knowledge tier — ratings inherited from the Model Page's pattern-data-tier ratings. N57D30: Provisional-newly-assigned tier — no Model Page row exists for this code specifically for the E93; the rating here is derived from the established reputation of the single-turbo N57 in the E90 LCI and F30 applications.

overview.intro: The E93 is the convertible body of BMW's fifth-generation 3 Series, launched in December 2006 as the final variant of the E9X family. It introduced a folding metal hardtop — a first for the 3 Series convertible — replacing the fabric roof of the E46 convertible. A significant March 2010 facelift (LCI) brought more than cosmetic changes: it replaced the N54 twin-turbo petrol with the single-turbo N55 and the M57 diesel six with the all-new N57, splitting this generation's ownership story into two distinct mechanical eras under one body code. The E93 is also approximately 200 kg heavier than the E92 coupé due to the folding roof mechanism, affecting performance and fuel economy.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E9x (shared with E90 saloon, E91 Touring, E92 Coupé) • Predecessor: E46 Convertible • Successor: F33 4 Series Convertible • Sibling body styles in this generation: E90 Saloon, E91 Touring, E92 Coupé.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E9X family generated 256 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with E93-specific enquiries concentrated on the N47 diesel four and N54/N55 petrol six as these cars exit warranty and major component replacement decisions become critical.

items.quote: Cast-iron block, no rear-mounted timing chain to worry about, and a documented reputation for exceeding 300,000 miles with routine servicing. Thermostat housings and swirl flaps are its only real demands. In the E93, it's the diesel you can buy without anxiety.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the last of BMW's bulletproof diesel-six era.

items.quote: Naturally aspirated, no HPFP, no turbo — just smooth, linear six-cylinder power with VANOS/Valvetronic as the only real wear items. In the heavier E93 convertible, the N52's 272 bhp is perfectly matched to the chassis.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who want simplicity over outright pace. (Model-wide: B58B30 in the G20 sets the modern benchmark — see G20 →)

items.quote: The rear-mounted timing chain is this generation's defining diesel risk — cold-start rattle is the warning, and a snapped chain means full replacement. The E93's weight and automatic transmission options don't help; the N47 is the single biggest ownership risk on the convertible diesel range.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented chain-replacement history.

items.quote: High-pressure fuel pump failure leads to long cranks and limp mode on the twin-turbo 335i; injector issues often follow. In the E93 convertible, the additional body weight means the N54 works harder, potentially accelerating component wear.
items.whoItsFor: 335i buyers with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: Older than the N57, but proven, cheaper to insure on higher mileage, and free of the rear-chain question entirely. In the E93, the 231 bhp six-cylinder diesel offers effortless waftability with the roof down.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder torque without the modern diesel's caveats.

items.quote: Genuinely improved over the M57 on economy and refinement, and — critically for this specific generation — E93 never received the later twin-turbo N57 variants whose crank-bearing risk affects F30/G30-era cars. E93's N57 is the single-turbo version only.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern diesel-six without the twin-turbo risk profile that shows up in later cars.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — 272 bhp, no turbo worries
Most Expensive — fast, but costly to maintain
Risk vs Reward — budget for chain history
Solid Choice — improved economy, single-turbo only

economicsRule.title: The E93 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £5,000–£15,000 E93 sits at 21–110% of the car's value. On a sub-£6,000 E93, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £12,000+ low-mileage example, the same recon drops to a routine 27–46% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. Remember: E93 convertibles command a premium over E90 saloons, so the threshold calculation differs.

problems.title: N47D20A/C Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The rear-mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time — cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. This affects the entire N47 family in the E93.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on any N47-powered E93, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E93 worth under £6,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your E93 320d →

problems.title: N54B30 HPFP & Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The high-pressure fuel pump's internal wear leads to fuel pressure loss, hard starting, and eventual engine damage if ignored; injector issues frequently follow. The E93's additional 200 kg weight over the E92 means the N54 works harder, potentially accelerating wear.
problems.recommendation: If your E93 335i has no documented HPFP history, treat it as overdue preventative maintenance — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows a lean-running failure. This is the single most important maintenance item on the N54.

cta.label: Compare N54 engine prices for your E93 335i →

problems.title: M57D30 Thermostat & Swirl Flap Wear
problems.rootCause: Plastic thermostat housings crack with age and heat cycling; swirl flap linkages in the intake wear and can, in rare cases, detach into the cylinder. The E93's weight doesn't affect this directly, but the engine works harder in the heavier convertible.
problems.recommendation: This is cheap insurance on the E93's most dependable diesel engine — a £200–£400 thermostat and swirl flap check every service is trivial against the M57's otherwise bulletproof reputation. In the E93, it's the smartest preventative spend you can make.

cta.label: Compare M57 engine prices for your E93 330d →

problems.title: Folding Hardtop Mechanism Wear
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on this generation — the folding metal hardtop's hydraulic system and microswitches can fail with age, leading to roof operation faults. The E93's roof is heavy and complex, and repairs can be expensive.
problems.recommendation: Have the roof mechanism inspected at every major service on any E93 over 80,000 miles — a £300–£700 preventative service is dramatically cheaper than the £1,200–£2,500 full overhaul that follows if ignored. This is the single most common reason an otherwise sound E93 fails an inspection.

cta.label: Compare E93 roof mechanism repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: E93 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E93 convertible only. E90 saloon, E91 Touring and E92 Coupé share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary by body style — particularly in the convertible, where additional structural bracing can complicate engine access. The E93 M3 is covered separately on its own generation page.
